content:
# Romans Chapter 14 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ROM_014_page_001.jpg

1. As for the one who is weak in faith, welcome him, but not to quarrel over opinions.

2. One person believes he may eat anything, while the weak person eats only vegetables.

3. Let not the one who eats despise the one who abstains, and let not the one who abstains pass judgment on the one who eats, for God has welcomed him.

4. Who are you to pass judgment on the servant of another? It is before his own master that he stands or falls. And he will be upheld, for the Lord is able to make him stand.

5. One person esteems one day as better than another, while another esteems all days alike. Each one should be fully convinced in his own mind.

6. The one who observes the day, observes it in honor of the Lord. The one who eats, eats in honor of the Lord, since he gives thanks to God, while the one who abstains, abstains in honor of the Lord and gives thanks to God.

7. For none of us lives to himself, and none of us dies to himself.

8. For if we live, we live to the Lord, and if we die, we die to the Lord. So then, whether we live or whether we die, we are the Lord's.

9. For to this end Christ died and lived again, that he might be Lord both of the dead and of the living.

10. Why do you pass judgment on your brother? Or you, why do you despise your brother? For we will all stand before the judgment seat of God;

11. for it is written, "As I live, says the Lord, every knee shall bow to me, and every tongue shall confess to God."

12. So then each of us will give an account of himself to God.

13. Therefore let us not pass judgment on one another any longer, but rather decide never to put a stumbling block or hindrance in the way of a brother.

14. I know and am persuaded in the Lord Jesus that nothing is unclean in itself, but it is unclean for anyone who thinks it unclean.

15. For if your brother is grieved by what you eat, you are no longer walking in love. By what you eat, do not destroy the one for whom Christ died.

16. So do not let what you regard as good be spoken of as evil.

17. For the kingdom of God is not a matter of eating and drinking but of righteousness and peace and joy in the Holy Spirit.

18. Whoever thus serves Christ is acceptable to God and approved by men.

19. So then let us pursue what makes for peace and for mutual upbuilding.

20. Do not, for the sake of food, destroy the work of God. Everything is indeed clean, but it is wrong for anyone to make another stumble by what he eats.

21. It is good not to eat meat or drink wine or do anything that causes your brother to stumble.

22. The faith that you have, keep between yourself and God. Blessed is the one who has no reason to pass judgment on himself for what he approves.

23. But whoever has doubts is condemned if he eats, because the eating is not from faith. For whatever does not proceed from faith is sin.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Romans 14 addresses the most practical issue in the Roman church: how do believers with different convictions about food and holy days live together? Paul's answer establishes principles that extend far beyond the first-century dispute.

**The pastoral context:** LOGOS identifies the "weak" as likely Jewish Christians maintaining Torah observance and the "strong" as those (including Paul) who consider all foods clean. PNEUMA captures the lived reality: "what to serve at potluck dinners." SOPHIA affirms: this is not a trivial issue. In the ancient world, shared meals were the primary expression of community. If you can't eat together, you can't be church together. Paul's solution is not uniformity of practice but unity of love.

**Verses 7-9 — The lordship principle:** SOPHIA affirms: this is the theological center of the chapter. Because Christ is Lord of both living and dead, both the one who eats and the one who abstains belong to Christ. Judgment belongs to the Lord, not to brothers. The practical implication: stop evaluating each other's spirituality based on secondary issues.

**Verse 17 — The kingdom definition:** PNEUMA reads it as Paul's version of "the Sabbath was made for man." SOPHIA affirms: this verse relativizes all external religious practices. The kingdom of God is not defined by what you eat or don't eat, which days you observe or don't observe. It is defined by righteousness, peace, and joy in the Holy Spirit. This is Paul's most ecumenical statement — it establishes a hierarchy of values in which Spirit-produced character always trumps ritual observance.

**The love-liberty principle (vv.13-21):** LOGOS captures the paradox: "freedom is real, but love is higher." SOPHIA affirms: Paul simultaneously holds two truths: (1) all foods are clean (v.14, v.20); (2) love may require not eating certain foods (v.15, v.21). Freedom without love is destructive; love without freedom is legalism. The mature believer exercises freedom voluntarily constrained by love. This is Christian liberty in its fullest expression.

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
